input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The Physical Twin is a 1/10-scale autonomous vehicle (as depicted in Fig. 1) with base hardware consisting of a teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced sensor and control technology. Main hardware layers include lower and upper level chassis and autonomy elements. The operational domain is Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) within Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS), characterized by resource-constrained electronic control units (ECUs) necessitating offloading of computation for safety-critical operations.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides three core services: (1) Data Monitoring via a user interface (UI) that displays real-time vehicle state data, projected driving path, and detected object tracks while enabling data saving and report generation; (2) Computation Offloading where the ACC system (including lead vehicle detection, speed calculation based on distance, and actuation commands) is offloaded from on-board ECUs to cloud infrastructure; (3) Remote Management allowing operators to enable/disable ACC, set desired speed, and send emergency braking commands from remote locations. Target users include operators for real-time system management and developers for troubleshooting using data collection capabilities.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: NVIDIA Jetson NX antenna, NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano, Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR, NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0, Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2), Python 3.8, C++, and MATLAB/Simulink for hardware and software implementation, supporting low-latency communication, data collection, and real-time processing for the ACC-DT system.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The system requires 100 ms clock synchronization accuracy for computation offloading between physical and digital components due to hardware limitations, despite safety-critical vehicular standards mandating stricter synchronization (less than 100 ms) to ensure temporal consistency.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The architecture follows ISO 23247 RA with three distinct entities (User, Digital Twin, Physical Twin) deployed on separate hardware components. Each entity's functionality is implemented as individual ROS2 packages containing one or more ROS2 nodes, with the User Interface FE split into 3D Visualization and Control Panel sub-components. The Physical Twin Entity handles resource-specific functions like Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking, while the Digital Twin Entity manages data processing and control mechanisms.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: An entity-based architecture mapped to ISO 23247 RA, where each entity (User, Digital Twin, Physical Twin) is deployed on dedicated hardware and coordinated via ROS2 packages and nodes. The Physical Twin Entity extends beyond standard OME to include general automotive functionality, ensuring seamless integration of sensors, actuators, and compute resources within the system.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Physical actuators include a Motor for propulsion control and a Steering Servo for directional adjustment. The Actuation component converts desired speed commands into voltage signals, with remote control interfaces via a Control Panel enabling Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) activation and emergency braking functions.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensing infrastructure comprises a Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR for 2D obstacle detection and an Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera for 3D spatial measurement. These sensors collect vehicle state metrics such as speed and steering angle, with data processed in real-time for system monitoring.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Raw sensor data from physical entities including LiDAR point clouds, vehicle state, motor, and steering servo are collected via Data Collecting FE and preprocessed by Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking and Data Pre-Processing FE components. Data Translation component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions. All data flows occur in real-time over a ROS2 network for internal component communication and UDP over Wi-Fi for inter-entity communication.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands generated by the ACC Controlling FE are serialized and translated via the Data Translation component, then transmitted over ROS2 for internal component communication and UDP over Wi-Fi for inter-entity communication to actuate motor and steering servo actuators.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Communication between software components occurs within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication uses UDP over Wi-Fi in the transport layer.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The Digital Twin supports decision-making through Data Monitoring services enabling real-time data viewing, saving, and report generation; Computation Offloading for Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) processing; and Remote Management functionalities to configure and control Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS) remotely. These capabilities are implemented via the User Entity and associated Functional Entities as defined in the ISO 23247 reference architecture.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Compliance with ISO 23247:2021 standard, which includes four parts: Overview and general principles, Reference architecture (Part 2), Digital representation of physical manufacturing elements, and Information exchange. The standard specifies an entity-based reference architecture with Observable Manufacturing Elements (OME), Data Collection and Device Control Entity, Digital Twin Entity, and User Entity. Interoperability is supported through protocols such as OPC Unified Architecture for Functional Entity implementation.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately describes the physical twin as a 1/10-scale autonomous vehicle, detailing its hardware layers (lower and upper chassis, autonomy elements) and the base teleoperated vehicle origin. It correctly identifies the operational domain as Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) within ADAS and highlights the issue of resource-constrained ECUs requiring computation offloading. Supported by quotes: 'case study of developing an Adaptive Cruise Control DT for a 1/10-scale autonomous vehicle', 'lower level chassis... upper level chassis... autonomy elements', and 'resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient'.

The extraction accurately captures the three core services described in the text: Data Monitoring via UI (real-time data, projected path, object tracks, reporting), Computation Offloading (ACC system moved to cloud infrastructure), and Remote Management (enable/disable ACC, set speed, emergency braking). It also correctly lists the target users (operators and developers). Supported by quotes: '1) Data Monitoring: ... user interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate reports', '2) Computation Offloading: ... offloading only the ACC system from the vehicle to the DT', and '3) Remote Management: ... enabling/disabling ACC, setting the desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command'.

The extraction lists the specific tools and technologies used: NVIDIA Jetson NX/Orin Nano, Hokuyo LiDAR, Jetpack 5.0, ROS2, Python 3.8, C++, and MATLAB/Simulink. It correctly associates them with hardware and software implementation. This is fully supported by the text: 'NVIDIA Jetson NX antenna and NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano microcomputer', 'Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR', 'NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0', 'Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2)', and 'Application modules are implemented in Python 3.8... C++... and MATLAB/Simulink'.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately captures the specific clock synchronization requirement of 100 ms, linking it to the need for computation offloading and noting the relaxation of stricter safety standards due to hardware constraints. This is directly supported by the system requirements defined in the text.

The extraction provides a highly detailed description of the system's multiplicities, correctly identifying the three ISO 23247 entities, their mapping to specific hardware components, the ROS2 implementation strategy, and the specific decomposition of the User Interface FE. It also correctly assigns the Point Cloud Clustering functionality to the Physical Twin Entity.

The extraction perfectly summarizes the architectural orchestration, describing the entity-based mapping to ISO 23247 RA and the coordination via ROS2 on dedicated hardware. It specifically highlights the key architectural adaptation where the Physical Twin Entity extends the standard OME to include general automotive resources.

The source text focuses on the internal architecture of the Digital Twin System (DTS), consisting of the User, Digital Twin, and Physical Twin entities, along with their internal communication protocols. It does not describe integration with external enterprise systems, other digital twins, or cross-domain data exchange protocols.

block_3_docs_retrieved:
In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

block_3_judge.reasoning: The characteristic is described as 'Not in Document'. The source text provides no specific details on computational models (geometric, behavioral, analytical), data sources, data management approaches, or model relationships within the Digital Twin constellation. While the text mentions 'Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking' and 'ACC' (Table I), these are described as software components or services, not as detailed models or data structures. Therefore, the statement is correct.

The extraction is highly specific and well-supported by the text. It correctly identifies the actuators (Motor, Steering Servo) and describes the Actuation component and remote control interfaces. Quotes: 'Actuation Converts desired speed into motor voltage commands' (Table I); 'Control Panel Interface for the user to send remote management commands' (Table I); 'enable/disable ACC... sending an emergency braking command' (Section III.A).

The extraction correctly identifies the specific sensor models and their general purpose from the text. Quotes: 'onboard sensors including a Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR, an Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera' (Section III.B). The claim that these sensors collect speed and steering angle is supported by system requirements (R2) stating the system shall collect these metrics, though the text implies 'Vehicle State' is a specific node, making the exact attribution to the LiDAR/Camera slightly inferred but consistent with the 'sensing infrastructure' description.

The characteristic is described as 'Not in Document'. The source text defines DTs generally as 'high-fidelity computational reflections' (Section I) but does not provide specific details on fidelity requirements, validation/verification methods, uncertainty quantification, or error detection mechanisms for the ACC Digital Twin. The focus is on architecture and services, not on model validation or quality assurance processes. Therefore, the statement is correct.

(a) 3D Visualization of the vehicle and its environment (b) Corresponding view from the on-board camera Fig. 3: Visualizations available from the virtual cockpit _C. Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer. _1) Data Monitoring:_ To enable data monitoring, ACCrelevant data such as vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path are displayed in real-time on the 3D Visualization, as shown in Fig. 3a. Raw point cloud data from the LiDAR sensor must be processed by the _Point Cloud_ _Clustering & Tracking_ SW component, which turns the raw point cloud, represented as individual points on a 2D plane,

block_4_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately captures the data flow from physical sensors to virtual components. It correctly identifies the specific data sources (LiDAR point clouds, vehicle state, motor, steering servo), the functional entities involved in preprocessing (Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking, Data Pre-Processing FE), the Data Translation component's role (serialization, message alignment, unit conversion), and the specific communication protocols (ROS2 for internal, UDP/Wi-Fi for inter-entity). This is strongly supported by the text regarding 'Data Collection', 'Data Translation', 'Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking', 'Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network', and the mention of sensors in the Physical Twin section.

The description accurately details the control flow from the digital twin (ACC Controlling FE) to the physical actuators (motor, steering servo). It correctly identifies the generation of commands by the 'ACC' component (mapped to Controlling FE in Table I), the translation step via 'Data Translation', and the transmission protocols (ROS2, UDP over Wi-Fi). The text supports this with 'Actuation Converts desired speed into motor voltage commands' (Table I), 'ACC Determines lead vehicle and computes following speed' (Table I), and the general communication architecture described in the Implementation of Services section.

The extraction correctly identifies the technical network infrastructure used for the system. It specifies ROS2 for internal software component communication and UDP over Wi-Fi for inter-entity communication. This is directly supported by the text: 'Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using U...'. While the text cuts off at 'using U...', 'U' clearly implies UDP, which is standard for such contexts and explicitly mentioned in the other extractions derived from the same source logic (likely consistent across the paper's full text or inferred from context, though strictly sticking to the provided snippet, 'using U...' is the evidence). However, the extraction is accurate to the provided snippets which clearly state ROS2 and Wi-Fi.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The provided source documents discuss the components (Physical Twin Entity, Digital Twin Entity, User Entity) and their software implementations (ROS2 packages) and hardware mapping (NVIDIA Jetson, etc.), but do not explicitly describe the deployment infrastructure in terms of cloud/edge/on-premise hosting models, scalability mechanisms, or specific deployment models beyond stating 'each entity has been deployed on a different hardware component'. There is no mention of cloud hosting platforms or detailed storage requirements for the infrastructure itself, though specific hardware for the physical twin is listed. Therefore, the detailed characteristics requested (hosting platforms, scalability models) are indeed not present in the text.

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.

block_5_judge.reasoning: The source text mentions that DTs can be used as a 'product lifecycle management tool' in automotive and distinguishes between the 'design phase' and 'vehicle operation' where DTs are applied. However, it does not define the specific lifecycle phases *of the Digital Twin itself*, nor does it describe transition management or lifecycle governance approaches. Therefore, the assessment that this information is 'Not in Document' is correct regarding the specific characteristic definition.

The document details the architecture and implementation of the ACC-DT case study, including requirements and software components. However, it does not describe the engineering methodologies used (e.g., specific development models), quality assurance practices, testing/validation approaches (beyond general case study context), version control, or evolution management strategies. Thus, stating this is 'Not in Document' is accurate.

The extraction accurately captures the decision support capabilities described in the text: Data Monitoring, Computation Offloading, and Remote Management. Supported by quotes: 'The DT presented in this case will contain a user interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate reports of vehicle state data'; 'offloading only the ACC system from the vehicle to the DT'; 'enable the management and operation of ACC remotely'.

The description is highly accurate and detailed, directly supported by the background section on ISO 23247. It correctly identifies the standard's four parts, the entity-based reference architecture (OME, Data Collection, DT, User), and the use of OPC Unified Architecture for interoperability.

block_6_judge.reasoning: The source documents focus on the engineering architecture, implementation, and requirements of an automotive Digital Twin. There is no mention of data ownership, privacy policies, consent management, or data sharing agreements. Therefore, the claim 'Not in Document' is correct.

While the documents mention the system is 'safety-critical' and requires low latency (R7) and clock synchronization (R8), they do not describe cybersecurity protocols, access control mechanisms, authentication systems, or threat mitigation strategies. The text mentions an 'emergency braking command' as a function but does not detail safety mechanisms or fail-safes. Since the extraction states 'Not in Document' and the sources lack detailed security and specific safety measures, the score is 5.
